title: Lab 6B: Acoustic Positioning System
preview: This lab stood out to me the most. It made me rethink how GPS works, but instead of satellites, we used sound. The moment the algorithm printed a real coordinate, it clicked.

detail.body:
This lab stood out to me the most in EECS16A. It made me rethink how GPS works, except instead of satellites bouncing signals off space, we used speakers and a microphone in a room. The idea is the same though: if you know how long a signal takes to arrive from multiple known points, you can figure out where you are.

The part that really clicked for me was cross-correlation. You play a known signal from each speaker, record what the microphone hears, and then find the delay by looking for the peak in R(t) = sum of x(n) times y(n + t). That peak tells you the time difference, which gives you distance via d = v times dt. With three speakers, you get three distances, and you solve for position using least squares: x = A plus times b. The moment Python printed an actual coordinate from just audio math, it felt like magic that made complete sense.
